steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: post-endoscopic esophageal/gastric perforation or iatrogenic injury; biliary pathology (choledocholithiasis given prior cholecystectomy); intra-abdominal abscess or infected fluid collection
ensemble.information_gap: The physician cannot yet characterize the extent, etiology, or complications of the abdominal process — specifically whether there is pancreatic necrosis, peripancreatic fluid, a perforation from the recent endoscopy, or another intra-abdominal source explaining the markedly elevated WBC (26.7 K/uL) and lipase (931 IU/L) with fever history
ensemble.expected_finding: Peripancreatic fat stranding, pancreatic edema or necrosis consistent with acute pancreatitis; alternatively, free air or extraluminal contrast suggesting esophageal/gastric perforation from the recent dilation procedure; or a fluid collection/abscess explaining the leukocytosis
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With troponin now negative (ruling out NSTEMI as the primary driver), a lipase >15× the upper limit of normal, WBC of 26.7 K/uL, history of recent endoscopic esophageal dilation, and post-procedural fevers, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate next step to characterize the severity of pancreatitis and exclude a perforation or other surgical emergency.
grounding: History: endoscopy one week ago for recurrent esophageal strictures, after which he has been having increasing pain as well as intermittent fevers
History: lipase of 700 at outside hospital, transferred for concern for NSTEMI
Physical examination: mild TTP in the epigastric area, ND, no rebound, no guarding
CT chest/neck with contrast showed no acute abnormalities in either the neck or chest
ensemble.reasoning: Now that the troponin has trended down to undetectable and the cardiac workup (EKG, CK-MB, troponin) is reassuring against NSTEMI, the dominant clinical problem shifts to the markedly elevated lipase and profound leukocytosis in the setting of a recent endoscopic esophageal dilation. I need CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast to determine whether this is straightforward acute pancreatitis, complicated pancreatitis with necrosis or fluid collections, or a post-procedural perforation/mediastinal extension not captured on the outside chest CT — all of which would substantially change management.

Physical examination: mild TTP in the epigastric area, ND, no rebound, no guarding
CT chest/neck with contrast showed no acute abnormalities in either the neck or chest

verification.actual_finding: CT showed fatty atrophy of the pancreas with mild peripancreatic fat infiltration surrounding the pancreatic head consistent with early acute pancreatitis, without necrosis, pseudocyst, free air, perforation, or other intra-abdominal source of infection.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The finding of peripancreatic fat infiltration directly matches the physician's expected finding of peripancreatic fat stranding/pancreatic edema consistent with acute pancreatitis, and the absence of free air or fluid collection rules out the alternative perforation/abscess hypotheses.
